description: Local backup data for Indian wildlife species - used when external APIs fail
source: Wikipedia, IUCN Red List, Wikimedia Commons

species.description: The Bengal tiger is a population of the tiger subspecies native to the Indian subcontinent. It is the world's largest living cat species, known for its distinctive orange coat with black stripes. Found primarily in India and Bangladesh, with small populations in Nepal and Bhutan.
species.habitat: Tropical and subtropical forests, mangroves, grasslands
funFacts: Can consume up to 40 kg of meat in one sitting
Individual tigers can be identified by their stripe patterns

species.description: The Asian elephant is the sole remaining species of the genus Elephas. Native to South and Southeast Asia, these intelligent creatures inhabit grasslands, forests, and wetlands. They are highly social animals living in matriarchal herds.
species.habitat: Grasslands, tropical evergreen and deciduous forests
funFacts: Have the longest pregnancy of any land mammal (22 months)

species.description: The Indian leopard is one of the largest leopard subspecies, found throughout the Indian subcontinent. Known for its adaptability, it inhabits environments from rainforests to dry deciduous forests. Solitary and nocturnal, it is an opportunistic hunter.
species.habitat: Tropical rainforests, dry deciduous forests, arid regions
funFacts: Can drag prey larger than itself into trees
Can survive without water for extended periods

species.description: The lion-tailed macaque is a Old World monkey endemic to the Western Ghats of South India. Named for its distinctive silverish mane, it inhabits rainforest canopies and is primarily frugivorous. Highly intelligent, it uses tools in captivity.
funFacts: Spends 70% of time in tree canopy
Uses alarm calls to warn of different predators

species.description: The gaur is the largest extant wild cattle species, native to South and Southeast Asia. With a massive body and distinctive dorsal ridge, it inhabits hilly forests. Gaur are primarily grazers but also browse on herbs and shrubs.
funFacts: Slamming their horns against trees signals alarm

species.description: The sloth bear is a myrmecophagous bear species native to the Indian subcontinent. Distinctive for its long, shaggy fur and pale muzzle, it feeds primarily on termites and fruits. It carries cubs on its back, a unique trait among bears.
funFacts: Can seal termite mounds and create air shafts

species.description: The dhole is a wild dog native to South and Southeast Asia. Unlike other canids, dholes are highly social and live in packs of up to 12 individuals. Known for their cooperative hunting, they prey on deer, wild boar, and even tigers.
funFacts: Unique among canids - packs help raise pups
Can survive without water for months

species.description: The Indian pangolin is a solitary, nocturnal mammal covered in overlapping keratin scales. Found throughout India, it feeds almost exclusively on ants and termites using its long, sticky tongue that can extend up to 40 cm.
funFacts: Scales made of keratin (same as human nails)
Can roll into a ball for protection
One of the most trafficked mammals globally

species.description: The Bengal fox is a fox endemic to the Indian subcontinent. Found primarily in the semi-arid grasslands of western India, it has a bushy tail with a black tip. Primarily nocturnal, it feeds on rodents, insects, and fruits.
funFacts: Mated pairs stay together for life
Digs its own burrows for shelter

species.description: The Himalayan black bear is a subspecies of the Asian black bear found in the Himalayan mountains. Distinguished by its white chest patch, it inhabits high-altitude forests. An omnivore, it feeds on fruits, nuts, insects, and occasionally livestock.
funFacts: Can make beds in trees for sleeping

species.description: The Indian giant squirrel is one of the largest squirrel species, endemic to India. With a striking tri-colored coat of maroon, black, and cream, it is an arboreal species rarely descending to the ground. Found in dense canopy forests.
funFacts: Can leap up to 6 meters between trees

species.description: The Himalayan musk deer is a small deer species found in the mountainous forests of the Himalayas. Known for producing musk from its scent gland, it is the source of the expensive perfume ingredient. Shy and solitary, it inhabits high altitudes.
funFacts: Lacks antlers - has instead a canine tooth

species.description: The red panda is a small arboreal mammal endemic to the Himalayas. Despite its name, it is not closely related to the giant panda. It has a distinctive reddish-brown fur, long, bushy tail, and feeds primarily on bamboo.
funFacts: Eats 30% of its body weight daily
Has a fake thumb (extended wrist bone)
Loves cold weather and hibernates in snow

species.description: The Indian peafowl, or blue peafowl, is the national bird of India. The male displays spectacular iridescent tail feathers during courtship. Found throughout the subcontinent, it inhabits open forest and farmland, feeding on grains, insects, and small vertebrates.
funFacts: Tail feathers can reach 6 feet long

species.description: The great hornbill is one of the largest hornbill species, found in the forests of India and Southeast Asia. Recognizable by its massive casque and black-and-white plumage, it feeds primarily on figs. Pairs mate for life and use tree cavities for nesting.
funFacts: Seals female in tree cavity during nesting
Eats hundreds of figs per day

species.description: The Indian eagle-owl is a large owl species endemic to the Indian subcontinent. With distinctive ear tufts and orange-yellow eyes, it inhabits rocky hills, scrublands, and forested areas. It preys on rodents, birds, and reptiles.
funFacts: Call sounds like a human scream

species.description: The sarus crane is the world's tallest flying bird, endemic to the Indian subcontinent. With grey plumage and red head, it mates for life and inhabits wetlands, marshes, and agricultural fields. Known for elaborate courtship dances.

The white-rumped vulture is a critically endangered vulture species endemic to South Asia. Once abundant, populations crashed by 99% due to diclofenac poisoning from livestock carcasses. Conservation breeding programs are attempting to save the species.

The Indian pitta is a brightly colored bird with green, blue, crimson, and black plumage. Found in forests across India, it is migratory, wintering in Sri Lanka. Known for its loud double-noted whistle, it feeds on insects and worms.
funFacts: Colorful but shy and hard to spot

species.description: The Asian woollyneck stork is a large wading bird found in South and Southeast Asia. With distinctive woolly white neck and black body, it inhabits wetlands, marshes, and flooded grasslands. It feeds on frogs, snakes, and fish.
funFacts: Symbol of good luck in some cultures

species.description: The Indian cobra is a venomous snake species native to the Indian subcontinent. Recognizable by the distinctive spectacle marking on its hood, it inhabits forests, fields, and villages. It preys on rodents, frogs, and birds. Symbol of snake charming tradition.
funFacts: Integral to Indian mythology and culture

species.description: Russell's viper is a venomous snake responsible for many snakebite deaths in India. With a distinctive pattern of reddish-brown oval spots, it inhabits open grasslands and agricultural fields. It is a leading cause of snakebite mortality worldwide.
funFacts: Leading cause of snakebite deaths in India

species.description: The Indian python is a large non-venomous snake species found in the Indian subcontinent. Growing up to 3 meters or more, it inhabits forests, grasslands, and marshes. It kills prey by constriction and feeds on mammals, birds, and reptiles.
funFacts: Listed in Schedule I of Wildlife Protection Act

species.description: The bamboo pitviper is a venomous pit viper species endemic to India. Bright green with red or yellow streaks, it is arboreal and nocturnal. Found primarily in bamboo forests, it feeds on frogs, lizards, and small mammals.

The gharial is a crocodilian species endemic to the Indian subcontinent. With its extremely narrow, elongated snout and bumpy bosses on the skull, it is uniquely adapted for fish hunting. Found in river systems of the Ganges and Brahmaputra basins.
funFacts: Males have ghara (pot) on snout tip

species.description: The Indian star tortoise is a beautiful tortoise species named for the star-like patterns on its shell. Found in the arid and semi-arid regions of India, it feeds on grasses, flowers, and fruits. Popular in the illegal pet trade, it is threatened by habitat loss.

The mugger is a medium-sized crocodilian found in the Indian subcontinent. Inhabiting marshes, lakes, and rivers, it is a opportunistic feeder consuming fish, birds, and mammals. Historically hunted for hides, it is now protected.

The purple frog is a fossorial species endemic to the Western Ghats. With a plump, purple body and tiny head adapted for burrowing, it spends most of its life underground. Only emerges for a few days each year to breed.
funFacts: Lives underground most of the year

species.description: The Indian purple marsh frog is a common frog species found throughout India. With purple-brown coloration and prominent eyes, it inhabits marshes, ponds, and rice paddies. It is an important predator of insects and serves as prey for snakes and birds.

The Kashmir cave eel is a poorly known amphibian species found in the caves of Kashmir. With elongated, eel-like body and reduced eyes, it is adapted to life in darkness. Very little is known about its biology due to its subterranean existence.

A small, brightly colored tree frog found in the Western Ghats. With golden-yellow coloration and adhesive toe pads for climbing, it inhabits wet forests and near water bodies. It feeds on small insects and is prey for snakes and birds.

The Indian bullfrog is one of the largest frogs in India, found throughout the subcontinent. With olive-green coloration and robust body, it inhabits wetlands, marshes, and agricultural fields. It is highly aggressive and will eat anything it can overpower.
funFacts: Sold for meat in some markets

species.description: The Indian giant flying squirrel is one of several flying squirrel species in India. With membrane stretched between limbs for gliding, it inhabits forest canopies and feeds on fruits, nuts, and insects. Nocturnal and solitary, it makes nests in tree hollows.

The small Indian civet is a small carnivore found throughout India. With greyish-brown coat and dark stripes, it inhabits forests, grasslands, and human settlements. Nocturnal and solitary, it feeds on small mammals, insects, and fruits.

The ruddy mongoose is a mongoose species found in India and Sri Lanka. With reddish-brown fur and black-tipped tail, it inhabits forests, rocky hills, and grasslands. It feeds on insects, small mammals, birds, and reptiles.

The Indian hare is a common hare species found throughout India. With brownish-grey fur and long ears, it inhabits grasslands, agricultural fields, and open forests. Nocturnal and crepuscular, it feeds on grasses, herbs, and crops.

The Indian wild boar is a robust suid found throughout India. With dark brown bristles and prominent tusks, it inhabits forests, grasslands, and agricultural fields. Omnivorous, it feeds on roots, tubers, insects, and small vertebrates.

The sambar deer is the largest deer species in India, found throughout the subcontinent. With dark brown coat and sturdy build, it inhabits forests and grasslands. It is a key prey species for tigers and other large predators.

The barking deer, or Muntjac, is a small deer species found in India. Named for its dog-like bark alarm call, it inhabits dense forests. Males have short antlers and elongated canine teeth. It is one of the oldest deer species.
funFacts: Males have antlers and canine teeth

species.description: The Indian wolf is a subspecies of grey wolf endemic to the Indian subcontinent. Smaller than its European cousin, it inhabits grasslands and arid regions. It lives in small packs and preys on antelopes, rodents, and livestock.

The striped hyena is a scavenger found in arid regions of India. With distinctive striped fur and sloping back, it is primarily nocturnal. It feeds on carcasses, insects, and small vertebrates, playing an important ecosystem role.
funFacts: Important for ecosystem as garbage collector

species.description: The Asiatic lion is a lion subspecies endemic to Gujarat, India. Slightly smaller than African lions with a distinctive skin fold under the belly, it inhabits the Gir Forest. A population of only ~600 individuals survives in the wild.

The Nilgiri tahr is a mountain goat endemic to the Nilgiri Hills of Tamil Nadu. With shaggy dark coat and curved horns, it inhabits rocky cliffs and grassy slopes. Found only in small, isolated populations in the Western Ghats.

The Tibetan antelope, or chiru, is endemic to the Tibetan plateau. Males have long, slender black horns while females lack them. Found at high altitudes, it is threatened by poaching for its Shahtoosh wool, which cannot be legally traded.

The clouded leopard is a medium-sized cat found in the Himalayan forests of India. With cloud-like dark blotches on its coat, it is an excellent climber. One of the most secretive cats, it is rarely seen in the wild.
funFacts: One of rarest cats in India

species.description: The marbled cat is a small wild cat found in the forests of North-East India. With blotched fur pattern similar to clouded leopard but smaller, it is arboreal and feeds on birds, rodents, and reptiles. One of the least studied cats.

The fishing cat is a medium-sized cat native to South Asia. Adapted for wetland habitats, it has partial webbing between its toes and catches fish, frogs, and crustaceans. Found in marshes, mangroves, and wetlands throughout India.

The jungle cat is a medium-sized cat found throughout India. With uniform sandy-brown coat and black-tipped ears, it inhabits wetlands, grasslands, and forests. Unlike most cats, it is comfortable in water and hunts fish.

The Malabar giant squirrel is one of the largest squirrel species in the world. With a striking coat of maroon, black, and cream, it is endemic to the Western Ghats. It is primarily arboreal and feeds on fruits, nuts, and insects.

The brown palm civet is a civet species endemic to the Western Ghats of India. With brownish coat and prehensile tail, it is arboreal and nocturnal. It feeds on fruits, flowers, and insects, playing a role in seed dispersal.

The Bengal slow loris is a prosimian primate found in North-East India. With large, forward-facing eyes for nocturnal vision and slow movements, it feeds on fruits, insects, and tree sap. Threatened by habitat loss and illegal pet trade.

The Assam macaque is a Old World monkey found in North-East India and Southeast Asia. With brownish-grey fur and distinctive face, it inhabits forests and sometimes human settlements. It feeds on fruits, seeds, insects, and leaves.

The western hoolock gibbon is a gibbon primate found in North-East India. With blackish fur (males) or buff-colored fur (females), it is among the few apes in India. It inhabits dense forests and feeds on fruits, leaves, and insects.

The capped langur is a langur primate found in North-East India and Bangladesh. With dark fur, pale face, and distinctive cap, it inhabits forests and feeds primarily on leaves. It lives in groups and is threatened by habitat loss.

The lion-tailed macaque is an Old World monkey endemic to the Western Ghats. Named for its silverish mane, it is one of the most endangered primates in India. It inhabits rainforest canopies and feeds on fruits, seeds, and insects.

The black ibis is a large wading bird native to the Indian subcontinent. With glossy black plumage and red patch on the back of the head, it inhabits wetlands, marshes, and agricultural fields. It feeds on insects, frogs, and small reptiles.

The pied kingfisher is a distinctive black-and-white kingfisher found throughout India. With a characteristic crest and hovering hunting technique, it hovers over water before diving for fish. It inhabits rivers, lakes, and coastal areas.

The Indian roller, or neelkanth, is a brightly colored bird found in India. With turquoise-blue plumage and deep purple wing patches, it inhabits open forests and cultivated areas. It is the state bird of several Indian states.

The painted stork is a large wading bird found in wetlands across India. With white plumage, black wing tips, and pink neck, it has a distinctive down-curved bill. It feeds on fish and amphibians in shallow waters.

The black-winged stilt is a wading bird with extremely long legs. With black-and-white plumage and pink legs that can exceed 30 cm, it inhabits wetlands, marshes, and coastal areas. It feeds on insects, crustaceans, and small fish.

The common Indian krait is a highly venomous snake found throughout India. With black or bluish-black body with narrow white crossbands, it inhabits fields, forests, and villages. Nocturnal, it feeds on other snakes, rodents, and frogs.

The saw-scaled viper is a small but highly venomous snake found in arid regions of India. With reddish-brown body and distinctive serrated scales that make a sizzling sound when threatened. It inhabits scrublands and agricultural fields.
funFacts: One of most dangerous snakes in India

species.description: The Bengal monitor is a large lizard found throughout India. With greyish-brown body and long neck, it can grow up to 1.5 meters. It inhabits forests, grasslands, and human settlements. It feeds on insects, small mammals, and carrion.
funFacts: Hisses and lashes tail when threatened

species.description: The fan-throated lizard is a small lizard found in South India. Males have a distinctive fan-like throat pouch that can be extended for courtship displays. It inhabits rocky areas and dry forests, feeding on insects.

The house gecko is a common gecko found throughout India. With padded toes for climbing walls and distinctive chirping call, it inhabits human dwellings and buildings. It feeds on insects attracted to lights.

metadata.coverage: Indian wildlife species with verified data
metadata.dataQuality: Verified - real scientific names and accurate descriptions
